title: Garter-stitch scarf
subtitle: The first finished object every knitter makes.
excerpt: A 180 cm × 22 cm garter-stitch scarf in aran-weight wool. Cast on 40 stitches, knit every row until the ball runs out, cast off. The canonical beginner project — long enough to teach the knit stitch in the hand, short enough to finish in a week or two of evenings.
sourceNotes:
Drawn from two public-domain references; the garter-stitch scarf is the most-reproduced beginner pattern in the entire knitting literature, and the version below is the standard form rather than any one named pattern:

• Weldon's Practical Knitting, 7th series (1880s), Internet Archive — §Plain Scarf in garter stitch. The cast-on count and finished length below follow Weldon's standard adult scarf.
• Beeton's Book of Needlework (1870), §Knitted comforter — Beeton's comforter is functionally the same garment in a slightly heavier yarn; the structural advice on edge treatment matches.
• Craft Yarn Council Standard Yarn Weight System for the aran-weight gauge reference.

knitting.gaugeText: 18 sts × 28 rows = 10 × 10 cm in garter stitch on 5 mm needles, blocked.

recipeTools.notes: 5 mm (UK 6 / US 8) straight needles, at least 30 cm long

glossaryTerms.definition: The most-used cast-on. Pulls about three times the cast-on length of yarn off the ball as a tail, drapes it over the thumb and the working yarn over the index finger, and forms the first row of stitches by alternating between the two strands.
Knit every row, every side. Produces bumpy horizontal ridges identical on both faces of the fabric; the first-project beginner fabric.

The final row that locks the stitches off the needle so the work doesn't unravel. UK term; US: bind off. The standard method knits two stitches, then passes the first over the second, until one stitch remains; cut and pull through.
Threading the cast-on tail and the cast-off tail back through the fabric with a darning needle so they don't show and don't pull out. About 5 cm of zigzag stitching through the wrong side is enough.

content.text: This is the canonical beginner project. A 180 cm × 22 cm scarf in aran-weight wool, knit in
garter stitch

on 5 mm needles. The pattern is one row, repeated until the scarf is the length you want: knit every stitch. No purling, no shaping, no chart. The whole point is the knit stitch in the hand, repeated until the hand stops thinking about it.

Worked in evenings, this is about a week of viewing — an hour or two a night, somewhere between six and ten evenings depending on the speed of your hands. The finished scarf is generous for an adult; if you'd rather a shorter scarf, stop when the work measures the length you want. The cast-on width determines the scarf width; the ball of yarn determines roughly the length.
Materials

items.name: Yarn
items.qty: 200 g (about 440 m / 480 yards) of aran-weight yarn
items.substitutions: Any aran-weight wool, wool blend, or acrylic. Cotton works for a summer scarf but loses the drape. A heavier yarn (chunky) on the same needles produces a chunkier scarf at the cost of length.

items.name: Needles
items.qty: 5 mm (UK 6 / US 8) straight needles, at least 30 cm long
items.substitutions: If your gauge runs tight, go up to 5.5 mm; if it runs loose, drop to 4.5 mm. Match the swatch, not the stated size.

content.text: Gauge

18 stitches and 28 rows to 10 cm over garter stitch on 5 mm needles, after a light wet-block. Gauge matters less for a scarf than for a fitted garment — the scarf will be a scarf either way — but it sets the finished width. A loose gauge (16 stitches to 10 cm) produces a wider, drapier scarf; a tight gauge (20 stitches to 10 cm) produces a narrower, denser one. Skip the swatch and you might end up with a 30 cm-wide scarf instead of a 22 cm one.

To swatch, cast on 22 stitches and work in garter stitch for 30 rows. The swatch should measure roughly 12 cm × 11 cm. Wet-block it lightly (soak in cool water for ten minutes, squeeze out, lay flat to dry overnight) and then measure 10 cm across the middle. Count the stitches in that 10 cm. If you have 18, you're at gauge. If 16, go down to 4.5 mm needles. If 20, go up to 5.5 mm.
Abbreviations
Pattern
Using the
long-tail cast-on
, cast on 40 stitches. Pull off about 240 cm of yarn as the tail before you start; the long-tail cast-on uses roughly 6 cm of yarn per stitch and 40 stitches needs 240 cm.
Row 1 and every subsequent row: knit every stitch across the row.

There's no row count; you knit until the scarf is the length you want. Measure with a tape measure laid flat, not stretched. The pattern's 180 cm finished length is the standard adult scarf — long enough to wrap once and have both ends fall over the front of a coat. Aim for between 160 cm and 200 cm depending on the height of the wearer.
When the scarf is the length you want,
cast off

all stitches. The standard cast-off: knit two stitches, then pass the first stitch over the second and off the needle. One stitch is now on the right needle. Knit one more, pass the previous over again. Continue until one stitch remains; cut the yarn leaving a 15 cm tail and pull the tail through the last stitch.

The cast-off should match the cast-on in tension. If your cast-off is much tighter than the cast-on, the scarf will flare at the bottom; cast off looser by using one size larger needle in the right hand. If much looser, cast off with one size smaller needle.
Finishing
Thread the cast-off tail onto the darning needle and
weave in ends

through the wrong side of the fabric. Run the tail back and forth for about 5 cm, changing direction once or twice, then trim. Repeat with the cast-on tail. Garter stitch hides ends well — once woven in, neither tail should show on either face of the scarf.

Wet-block the finished scarf to soften the fabric and even out any tension differences row to row. Soak in cool water with a tablespoon of wool wash for ten minutes, squeeze out the water (don't wring), roll in a clean towel and step on it to remove the last of the water, then lay flat on a clean surface to the finished dimensions. Leave to dry overnight. The scarf gains 5–10% in length from the blocking; aim for the cast-on row to sit at 180 cm.
Variations
Chunky weight: substitute a chunky yarn and 6 mm needles. Cast on 32 stitches. About 250 g for the same finished length; the scarf knits up faster but uses more yarn.
DK weight: substitute a DK yarn and 4 mm needles. Cast on 50 stitches. About 200 g for the same length; the finished scarf is lighter and drapier.

Cotton summer scarf: substitute a DK cotton or cotton-linen blend and 4 mm needles. Cast on 50 stitches. Cotton has no spring; the finished scarf is heavier and less stretchy, which suits a summer scarf worn open. Don't block cotton — it loses shape.
Shorter scarf: cast on the same 40 stitches and stop at 130–150 cm. A shorter scarf is a child's or short-adult scarf; the cast-on width stays the same.
